items.q: What is the difference between vacuum forming and injection moulding?
items.correct: Vacuum forming sucks a heated thermoplastic sheet down over a mould; injection moulding forces molten plastic into a closed mould under pressure.
items.misconception: They are the same process carried out at different temperatures.
items.random_wrong: Vacuum forming is only used for shaping metals.
items.why: Both shape plastics with moulds, so students merge them. Vacuum forming makes thin shell forms from sheet; injection moulding makes solid, detailed parts from molten granules.

items.correct: No - sanding removes material as dust, so it is a wasting/finishing process; forming reshapes material without removing any waste.
items.misconception: Yes - any process that changes the shape of a material is forming.
items.random_wrong: Yes - sanding is a type of joining process.
items.why: Students label anything that alters shape as forming. The key test is whether waste material is removed: if it is, the process is wasting, not forming.

items.q: Are pop rivets a temporary joining method?
items.correct: No - rivets are permanent; they must be drilled out and destroyed to separate the parts.
items.misconception: Yes - any mechanical fastener without glue counts as temporary.
items.random_wrong: Yes - rivets are a type of adhesive.
items.why: Students assume mechanical equals temporary. The real test is whether the joint can be undone without damage: screws can, rivets and welds cannot.

items.q: Which of these is a wasting (material removal) process?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Drilling. “Vacuum forming” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Vacuum forming is most suitable for shaping which material?
items.why: It's Thermoplastic sheet. “Solid steel bar” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Machine screws with nuts. “Welding” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Why is injection moulding suited to high-volume production?
items.correct: After the high initial mould cost, each part is produced quickly, cheaply and consistently
items.misconception: The mould is very cheap to make
items.random_wrong: No surface finish is ever needed
items.why: The answer examiners want is: After the high initial mould cost, each part is produced quickly, cheaply and consistently. “The mould is very cheap to make” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A CNC router cuts 12 identical parts per hour. How many hours are needed to cut 60 parts?
items.why: The real figure is 5 hours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
